job_summary: 3,200 sf commercial restaurant tenant improvement in Dallas, TX for a change of occupancy from B to A-2 assembly, including Type I kitchen hood installation, grease interceptor, ADA restroom and path-of-travel upgrades, sprinkler modifications, fire/life-safety review, and outdoor patio seating. This triggers a commercial building permit for the change of use/interior alteration, plus separate mechanical, plumbing, electrical, and fire protection reviews/permits as applicable. Outdoor patio seating may also trigger zoning/site review and possibly a separate patio/encroachment approval depending on whether it is on private property or public right-of-way.

permits_required.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Interior Alteration / Change of Occupancy (B to A-2)
permits_required.portal_selection: Commercial Building Permit - Interior Alteration / Change of Occupancy
permits_required.notes: Required for the tenant improvement, occupancy change from B to A-2, ADA path of travel upgrades, and any code-triggered life-safety/building work. Dallas will review occupancy, egress, accessibility, and any structural or rated assembly impacts under the commercial building permit.

permits_required.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required.notes: Required for the Type I kitchen hood, exhaust duct, make-up air, and any associated mechanical equipment or penetrations. Fire/life-safety and grease-laden vapor exhaust details are typically reviewed with this permit.
Required for the grease interceptor and any associated sanitary drainage modifications serving the restaurant kitchen.

permits_required.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — ADA Restroom Fixture Alteration
permits_required.notes: Required if restroom fixtures, water closets, lavatories, floor drains, or waste/vent piping are being added, moved, or replaced as part of the ADA restroom upgrade.
Required for any new circuits, lighting, receptacles, hood controls, equipment connections, emergency/egress lighting, or panel modifications tied to the restaurant buildout.

permits_required.portal_selection: Fire Protection Permit - Fire Sprinkler Modification
permits_required.notes: Required for sprinkler head relocations, coverage changes, hydraulic recalculation, or any fire protection work affected by the new restaurant layout and occupancy change.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Interior Alteration / Change of Occupancy (B to A-2)
permits_required_logic.included_because: The scope explicitly includes a change of occupancy from B to A-2 assembly and ADA path of travel upgrades, which are building-code items handled under the commercial building permit and plan review.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Change of occupancy B to A-2, tenant improvement, ADA path of travel upgrades

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required_logic.included_because: A Type I kitchen hood is commercial mechanical equipment and requires mechanical review for exhaust, make-up air, clearances, and penetrations.
Restaurants with commercial food prep require grease interceptor work when the kitchen drainage system is installed or modified.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — ADA Restroom Fixture Alteration
permits_required_logic.included_because: ADA restroom upgrades commonly involve fixture relocation or replacement, which triggers plumbing review when drains, vents, or water lines are changed.
Restaurant TI work typically includes lighting, receptacles, equipment connections, hood controls, and possibly panel/circuit changes.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Tenant improvement electrical work implied by restaurant buildout

permits_required_logic.included_because: The scope explicitly includes sprinkler modifications, which require fire protection review and permit.

apply_address: 320 E. Jefferson Blvd., Room 118, Dallas, TX 75203
fee_range: Fee Estimate: **$24,000-$42,500+** (structured floor — see breakdown). Components: ~$13,000 base permit + plan review (Dallas, TX commercial restaurant TI floor) × 1.1× jurisdiction multiplier + $3,500 change-of-occupancy adder + $5,500 hood-fire-suppression adder + $2,500 grease-interceptor adder + $4,000 fire-sprinkler-modify adder + $3,000 ada-path-of-travel adder. **Verify against current fee schedule before quoting.**

approval_timeline.simple: Over-the-counter or same-day only for very limited trade items; this project is a commercial TI and will not be same-day. Expect intake plus routed plan review.
approval_timeline.complex: 4-8 weeks for commercial plan review in Dallas, longer if fire/life-safety, accessibility, sprinkler, or occupancy review cycles back for corrections.

inspections.description: Inspector checks rated corridor and tenant separations, egress path widths, door swings, accessibility clearances, restroom layout, any wall framing changes, and that the new A-2 occupancy layout matches approved plans before concealment.
inspections.timing: Before drywall, ceiling closure, or covering any rated assemblies

inspections.description: Inspector verifies Type I hood duct routing, welded/sealed grease duct construction, hood clearances, make-up air provisions, exhaust termination, fire suppression coordination, and that penetrations through rated assemblies are protected.
inspections.timing: Before duct concealment and before hood system is energized

inspections.description: Inspector checks grease interceptor location and sizing, sanitary waste routing, venting, fixture rough-ins, ADA restroom fixture heights/clearances, and that any floor drain or waste line changes match approved plans.
inspections.timing: Before slab patching, wall closure, or fixture set

inspections.description: Inspector verifies branch-circuit sizing, receptacle placement, GFCI/AFCI where applicable, hood control wiring, emergency/egress lighting, panel schedule updates, and equipment disconnects if installed.
inspections.timing: Before insulation, ceiling closure, or final device installation

inspections.stage: Fire Sprinkler Rough-In / Hydrostatic as Applicable
inspections.description: Inspector or fire protection reviewer checks sprinkler head relocation, coverage changes, hydraulic calculations, obstruction issues from new hood or soffits, and that the modified system still provides code-compliant coverage for the A-2 layout.
inspections.timing: Before ceiling closure and before final occupancy

inspections.description: Inspector verifies final ADA path of travel, restroom accessibility, exit signage, emergency lighting, fire extinguisher placement, hood/fire suppression final sign-off, sprinkler final approval, and that the space matches the approved occupancy and occupant load.
inspections.timing: After all trades are complete and before opening to the public

inspection_booking: Schedule inspections through the Dallas permit portal at https://developdallas.dallascityhall.com/PermitDallas/ or through the inspection request function tied to the permit record. Dallas inspections are typically requested online; if you must call, use (214) 948-4480. For commercial TI work, book each trade inspection separately and give at least 24 hours advance notice; plan for longer lead time if fire sprinkler or life-safety inspections need coordination with multiple reviewers. Verify the permit is active and posted before requesting inspections.
license_required: Commercial contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the tenant/owner, and the appropriate Texas license number must appear on each trade application: commercial general contractor for the building permit, TDLR-licensed electrical contractor for electrical work, TSBPE-licensed master plumber for plumbing, and the appropriate mechanical/HVAC contractor for hood/mechanical work. The permit is still required; the licensed contractor is the one who submits and signs for the trade permit.
city_contractor_registration: Dallas requires contractors to register with the city each year. Verify the current Dallas contractor registration status before submitting any permit application; an expired or lapsed registration can block the application. Renewal is annual.
what_to_bring: Completed commercial permit application and project address/tenant suite information
Full plan set showing occupancy change, life-safety plan, ADA path of travel, restroom layout, hood plan, grease interceptor details, and sprinkler changes
Contractor license numbers and current Dallas contractor registration proof for each trade
Equipment cut sheets for the Type I hood, exhaust fan, make-up air unit, grease interceptor, and any electrical equipment
Code/occupant load and accessibility details, including door hardware, restroom clearances, and egress calculations
Owner/tenant authorization letter and lease exhibit if the contractor is filing on behalf of the tenant
common_mistakes: Submitting the permit application without verifying current Dallas contractor registration status — an expired or lapsed city registration will block your application silently.
Undervaluing the project on the permit application — Dallas uses a valuation system and can audit or assess penalty fees if the declared value is too low.
Forgetting that a B to A-2 occupancy change triggers full life-safety and accessibility review, not just trade permits.
Installing the Type I hood or grease duct before the mechanical permit and fire review are approved.
Assuming the sprinkler contractor can just 'field adjust' heads without a formal fire protection permit and approved plans.
Missing ADA path-of-travel upgrades outside the suite, including route to restrooms and accessible entry details.
pro_tips: Check your Dallas contractor registration renewal date before starting any permit application — registration lapses are one of the most common avoidable rejection reasons.
Use the permit valuation carefully and realistically; Dallas flags low valuations, and a clean valuation up front is faster than an audit later.
If the patio seating is on public right-of-way or affects sidewalks, ask about a separate encroachment or zoning approval before you submit the building permit.
Bring a coordinated life-safety sheet with occupant load, exit widths, restroom counts, and ADA route details; that cuts down correction cycles on A-2 restaurant TI reviews.
Have hood, grease interceptor, and sprinkler submittals coordinated before first intake so the plan reviewer is not waiting on trade-specific sheets.

code_citation.section: IBC 2021 Section 302.1 / 303.1 / 1001.2 and IRC/IBC permit authority via local code adoption; Dallas permit authority under local ordinance
code_citation.text: Occupancy classification shall be determined by the nature of the use of the building or space. Assembly Group A-2 includes food and drink consumption. A permit is required for construction, alteration, and change of occupancy as adopted by the local jurisdiction.

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Outdoor patio seating is added, expanded, or located in a way that affects site circulation, setbacks, or occupancy load.
companion_permits.required_if: Outdoor patio seating is added, expanded, or located in a way that affects site circulation, setbacks, or occupancy load
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Any portion of the patio seating or associated features is in public right-of-way.
companion_permits.required_if: Any portion of the patio seating or associated features is in public right-of-way
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

zoning_hoa_flag: Outdoor patio seating may be subject to Dallas zoning, site plan, and possibly right-of-way/encroachment rules. If the patio is visible from a historic district or overlay area, additional design review may apply. For a restaurant use, confirm occupant load, parking, and accessible route compliance before finalizing the patio layout.
disclaimer: Data sourced Apr 28, 2026. Always verify current requirements directly with your local building department before starting work. Permit fees and requirements change frequently.
confidence_reason: Verified city/trade data found from official sources. Needs review for: fee_range

code_section_source.title: [PDF] List of Post-Construction BMP Appendices - City of Lebanon, Indiana

required_documents_source.title: Post disaster response and mitigation of future losses proceedings ...

inspection_process_source.title: [PDF] List of Post-Construction BMP Appendices - City of Lebanon, Indiana

checklist: Verify your contractor license is current and valid in TX
Confirm your liability insurance is active (certificate may be required at permit office)
Have the property address and legal parcel number ready
Know the project value/cost — required on most permit applications
Type I commercial exhaust hood listed/labeled per UL 710 with NFPA 96 compliance and AHJ-approved capture velocity per IMC 507
ANSUL R-102 (or equivalent UL 300) hood suppression: post-install discharge test certificate + every-6-month cleaning + annual inspection per NFPA 96 §11
ANSUL discharge auto-shutoffs all hood gas + electric fuel sources per IMC 507.7 — verify with AHJ during witness test
Grease interceptor sized per IPC 1003.3.4 by drainage fixture units (4-comp + mop typically 50+ GPM hydromechanical OR 750–1,500 gal exterior gravity); located ≤25 ft developed length from fixtures
Makeup air system balanced to ±10% of hood exhaust per IMC 506.3.4; tempered if > 35°F differential to prevent backdraft
Indirect waste with air gap on dishwasher, ice maker, prep sink, and steam kettle per IPC 802; commercial dishwasher backflow prevention (RPZ) per IPC 608.13.2
Dedicated mop sink with hose bib in janitor closet (IBC 902.3 / local health code) — required before health dept sign-off
ADA fixture counts per IBC Table 2902.1 + 60 in turning radius + lavatory pipe wrap insulation per ADA §606.5 + mirror reflective edge ≤40 in AFF per ADA §603.3
Local health department food establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before Certificate of Occupancy (parallel filing — file early)
TABC / state liquor authority clearance if alcohol service — separate filing from building permit
Walk-in cooler envelope per IECC 403.10.1 (≥R-25 medium-temp panels, ≥R-32 freezer panels); door gasket seal verification at final
Existing sprinkler system reuse: hydraulic recalc for new layout + current 5-year cert per NFPA 25; new heads in cooking equipment area must be K-25+ per NFPA 13
Floor drains within 5 ft of kitchen equipment per IPC 411 with backwater valves where required
Fire alarm + emergency lighting per NFPA 72/101 with current battery capacity test on file
Change of occupancy memo (B/M → A-2): include occupant load + exits + restroom count + sprinkler/fire-alarm impacts per IEBC §1001.2/1001.3 + IBC §303.3 (Group A-2)

Maricopa County (or local equivalent) Environmental Services Department food-establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before opening — coordinate with City CO schedule (MCESD: maricopa.gov)

Backflow prevention on EVERY food-service water connection: carbonated beverage (ASSE 1022 dual check), chemical dispenser at mop/service sinks, hose bibbs, dish-machine/ice/RO/espresso, pre-rinse — testable assemblies certified annually
Outdoor patio / sidewalk dining: verify city zoning (use permit may apply, e.g. Phoenix ZO §1207); include patio in occupant load + exiting + accessible route + sprinkler/awning review

Phoenix-specific (or equivalent local rule): contractor must be AZ ROC licensed AND associated with the Phoenix SHAPE PHX permit account before submittal (A.R.S. Title 32 Ch. 10) — frequently-missed pre-submittal blocker

Realistic fee warning: a 3,200 sq ft restaurant TI in Phoenix typically lands $8K–$25K+ across plan review + permit + fire + utility + health + zoning. Reject token min-fee outputs; calculate from the live fee schedule before quoting
Occupancy reclassification: identify existing approved occupancy + proposed group; require code-official approval + new/updated Certificate of Occupancy (IEBC §§1001.1, 1001.2, 1001.3; IBC Ch. 3)
Height/area limit recheck: compare new occupancy hazard category to existing construction type, allowable area, frontage/sprinkler increases, fire-wall strategy (IEBC §1011.5; IBC Ch. 5)

Fire-resistance + separation reassessment: re-verify fire barriers, horizontal assemblies, exterior wall ratings/openings, shaft enclosures, mixed-occupancy separations for the new hazard category (IEBC §§1011.1, 1011.5.3, 1011.6/7; IBC Ch. 7)
Fire protection re-run: sprinkler, fire alarm, hood suppression, and monitoring requirements at the new occupancy + IBC/IFC Ch. 9 thresholds (IEBC §§1004.1, 1011.2; IFC Ch. 9)

Means of egress recalculation: occupant load + number of exits + egress capacity + travel distance + common path + dead ends + door hardware updated to new occupancy (IEBC §§1005.1, 1011.4; IBC Ch. 10)
Accessibility / path-of-travel trigger: alterations to primary-function areas trigger ADA upgrades to altered area + serving amenities (2010 ADA §202.4 + 28 CFR §36.403)
Mechanical ventilation/exhaust recheck against the new occupancy: IMC ventilation, commercial-kitchen exhaust + makeup air, hazardous exhaust, energy compliance (IEBC §1008.1; IMC Ch. 4-6 + §507)
Plumbing demand + fixture-count recheck: water supply, sanitary load, fixture count, food-handling waste, grease/oil interceptors, chemical-waste approval (IEBC §§1009.1-1009.4; IPC Ch. 4 + 10)

Identify altered primary-function area: if scope alters an area where major activity occurs, an accessible path of travel to the altered area is required unless technically infeasible / disproportionate (2010 ADA §202.4; 28 CFR §36.403(a))
20% disproportionality cap: ADA path-of-travel work exceeding 20% of alteration cost is disproportionate at the federal level, but lower-cost items must still be prioritized (28 CFR §36.403(f)(1))

Accessible exterior route: site arrival points + accessible parking/passenger loading + curb ramps + walking surfaces + ramps/landings + slope/cross-slope + door clearances to altered area (2010 ADA §§206.2.1, 206.4, 403, 404, 405, 406)

Restroom path + restroom upgrades: if restrooms serve the altered area, accessible route + door + turning space + lavatory + water closet + grab bars + clearances + signage (2010 ADA §§213, 603-606, 609, 703)
Parking + passenger loading: recalculate accessible-stall count + van space + access aisle + signage for affected parking serving the altered area (2010 ADA §§208, 502, 503; 28 CFR §36.403(e)(1))
Drinking fountains, phones, and signage along the path: tactile/visual signs, room ID, directional, fountain units, public phones if any (2010 ADA §§211, 216, 602, 703, 704)
Doors, thresholds, and hardware on the route: clear width + maneuvering clearance + opening force + thresholds + lever/operable hardware + protruding objects (2010 ADA §§303, 307, 309, 404)

Confirm the required license is active and matches the application: Commercial contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the tenant/owner, and the appropriate Texas license number must appear on each trade application: commercial general contractor for the building permit, TDLR-licensed electrical contractor for electrical work, TSBPE-licensed master plumber for plumbing, and the appropriate mechanical/HVAC contractor for hood/mechanical work. The permit is still required; the licensed contractor is the one who submits and signs for the trade permit.
Bring 2 copies of all documents to Dallas Development Services Department

rejection_patterns.fix: Renew at dallas.gov/dsd annually — expiration causes silent rejection

rejection_patterns.pattern: Project value underestimation flagged by plan reviewer
rejection_patterns.fix: Use contractor cost estimating software for accurate value — audits can add penalty fees

fee_calculator.note: Provide job_value to calculate an exact fee where formulas are available.

expert_notes.title: No statewide general-contractor license for residential work
expert_notes.note: Texas does not require general contractors, home-improvement specialists, or handyman services to hold a state license — licensing is handled by trade-specific boards (TDLR for electrical/HVAC, TSBPE for plumbing) and by individual cities/counties. Confirm the AHJ's local registration requirements (most large cities require contractor registration even though the state does not), and never assume a sister-state GC license carries over. Trade subs still must hold the specific TDLR/TSBPE license below.
expert_notes.applies_to: All residential GC and remodel work in Texas

expert_notes.title: TDLR Air Conditioning & Refrigeration Contractor license required for HVAC
expert_notes.note: Any HVAC contractor performing work in Texas must hold a TDLR Air Conditioning and Refrigeration Contractor license — application fee is $115 and the license must list the proper environment (Class A unlimited tonnage vs Class B ≤25 tons cooling / ≤1.5M Btu heating) and endorsement (cooling, heating, or commercial refrigeration). Verify the contractor's TDLR license number and class match the scope before pulling a mechanical permit; mismatched class is a common rejection reason.
expert_notes.applies_to: All HVAC installation, replacement, and alteration work

expert_notes.title: TSBPE plumbing license required before bidding or working
expert_notes.note: The Texas State Board of Plumbing Examiners requires a Journeyman or Master plumber license prior to bidding or completing any plumbing work — there is no de minimis dollar threshold like California's $500 rule. Licenses renew annually and require six hours of CPE per the Plumbing License Law; apprentices may only work under direct supervision. Confirm the responsible Master Plumber's license is active on the TSBPE lookup before submitting plumbing plans, since an expired license blocks permit issuance.

expert_notes.title: TDLR is the umbrella regulator for most building trades
expert_notes.note: TDLR licenses and regulates 39 industries including electricians, HVAC contractors, elevator/escalator, boilers, and industrialized housing — but plumbing sits with TSBPE, not TDLR. When a job involves multiple trades, expect parallel licensing checks at two separate state agencies plus the local AHJ's contractor-registration desk. Pull the TDLR license-search and TSBPE license-search results into the permit packet to pre-empt plan-check holds.

expert_notes.title: 2021 IRC/IBC/IMC/IFGC effective for industrialized housing as of July 1, 2024
expert_notes.note: The Texas Commission of Licensing and Regulation adopted the 2021 editions of the IRC, IBC, IMC, IFGC, and related codes for industrialized housing and buildings on May 21, 2024, with an effective date of July 1, 2024. Modular and IHB projects built or shipped after that date must conform; site-built projects follow the AHJ's locally-adopted edition (often still 2015 or 2018 IRC in smaller jurisdictions). Always confirm the local edition before drafting — Texas has no uniform statewide site-built code.
expert_notes.applies_to: Industrialized housing, modular, and IHB-stamped projects

expert_notes.title: SB 783 (eff. Sept 1, 2025) — energy code update path via SECO
expert_notes.note: Texas SB 783 took effect September 1, 2025 and directs the State Energy Conservation Office (SECO) to evaluate and adopt updated IECC editions for residential and commercial construction, with amendment authority. The last statutorily-adopted residential energy code was the 2015 IECC, so any project at the 2025-2026 boundary should confirm whether the AHJ has moved to the 2021 or 2024 IECC under SECO's updated rulemaking. Build energy-compliance documentation against the AHJ's currently-enforced edition, not the state's default.
expert_notes.applies_to: New residential and commercial construction energy compliance

expert_notes.title: HB 3167 platting shot clock — 30 days for municipal action
expert_notes.note: Under HB 3167 (Local Govt Code Chapter 212), municipalities must approve, approve with conditions, or disapprove a plat or plan within 30 days of filing — silence is deemed approval. Disapproval must be in writing with specific reasons tied to ordinance or statute, and the applicant has the right to a 15-day cure-and-resubmit. Use this clock as leverage when a city stalls a subdivision or replat needed for ADU or infill projects.
expert_notes.applies_to: Subdivision plats, replats, and site plans subject to municipal review

expert_notes.title: Development-review shot clock for permit applications
expert_notes.note: Recent Texas legislation extends shot-clock concepts beyond platting to broader development reviews, capping the time municipalities can take to act on permit-related submittals before approval is presumed or fee refunds are triggered. This narrows the window for cities to demand iterative resubmittals and gives applicants statutory grounds to escalate stalled residential permits. Document the application's complete-submittal date in writing so the clock is provable if escalation is needed.
expert_notes.applies_to: Municipal residential permit and development-review timelines

expert_notes.title: TDI Windstorm (WPI-8) certification required in First Tier coastal counties
expert_notes.note: The Texas Department of Insurance Windstorm Inspection Program applies to designated catastrophe areas — the 14 First Tier coastal counties (Aransas, Brazoria, Calhoun, Cameron, Chambers, Galveston, Jefferson, Kenedy, Kleberg, Matagorda, Nueces, Refugio, San Patricio, Willacy) plus parts of Harris east of SH 146. New construction, additions, and re-roofs in these areas need a WPI-8 / WPI-8-C certificate of compliance issued by an appointed engineer or TDI inspector to be eligible for TWIA windstorm coverage. This is a SEPARATE filing from the building permit and must be scheduled in phases (foundation, framing, roof) — missing an inspection phase voids the certificate.
expert_notes.applies_to: New construction, additions, and re-roofs in First Tier coastal counties

expert_notes.title: TWIA insurability hinges on windstorm certification
expert_notes.note: To bind a Texas Windstorm Insurance Association policy on a coastal property, the structure must be certified as meeting the windstorm-resistant building-code requirements for its tier — without a current WPI-8 (or post-2020 equivalent), the property is uninsurable through TWIA and most private carriers will follow suit. Schedule the engineer-of-record windstorm inspection BEFORE drywall closes the framing, since a missed framing inspection cannot be reconstructed after the fact and the homeowner loses coverage at closing.
expert_notes.applies_to: Coastal Texas residential construction needing windstorm insurance

expert_notes.title: Floodplain permit required separate from building permit
expert_notes.note: Texas participates in the NFIP through county and municipal floodplain administrators — any development (including fill, grading, accessory structures, and substantial improvements) within a SFHA Zone A/AE/VE requires a floodplain development permit in addition to the building permit, and substantial-improvement work (>50% market value) triggers full base-flood-elevation compliance for the entire structure. In Harris County and other large counties, the floodplain office is a separate desk from permitting; confirm the determination letter and elevation certificate are in the packet before submittal.
expert_notes.applies_to: Any work within a FEMA SFHA or local floodplain overlay

expert_notes.title: Municipally-owned utilities and electric co-ops opted out of retail choice
expert_notes.note: Per PUC rule, municipally-owned electric utilities and electric cooperatives have had the right since January 1, 2002 to opt out of competitive retail choice — most have. For new service or solar interconnection in muni/co-op territory (e.g., Austin Energy, CPS Energy, Pedernales EC, Sam Houston EC), follow the utility's own interconnection and net-metering tariff, not ERCOT/PUC retail rules. This is a parallel filing to the electrical permit and often has its own application, fee, and inspection schedule.
expert_notes.applies_to: New service, solar PV, and battery interconnection in muni/co-op territory

expert_notes.title: Co-op permit-number prerequisite for new electric service
expert_notes.note: Many Texas electric cooperatives — including Sam Houston EC and Heart of Texas EC — will not energize new construction until the contractor provides the local AHJ's permit number and a completed New Construction Application. Submit the co-op's service application in parallel with the building permit (not after final inspection) because meter-set lead times in rural co-op territory routinely run 4-8 weeks and can stall CO issuance.
expert_notes.applies_to: New residential service in electric-cooperative territory

expert_notes.title: ERCOT Chapter 25 rules govern PV/battery interconnection in competitive territory
expert_notes.note: In ERCOT competitive-retail areas (Oncor, CenterPoint, AEP TX, TNMP), distributed-generation interconnection follows PUC Substantive Rules Chapter 25 — the homeowner's TDU (not the REP) processes the interconnection agreement, and the project requires a signed IA before the meter is set to bidirectional. File the IA application as soon as the system design is final; TDU review can run 4-6 weeks and is independent of the city's electrical permit.
expert_notes.applies_to: Solar PV and battery interconnection in ERCOT competitive territory

hidden_triggers.title: Change of occupancy (B/S to A-2/M) can trigger sprinkler retrofit and IEBC review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Converting an office, warehouse, or retail tenant to assembly or mercantile crosses occupancy classifications. The new occupant load can cross IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds and require an IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis even when the construction work looks small.
likely_required_actions: Run IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis (Chapter 10)
Recompute occupant load and check IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds
Update accessible route and restroom/fixture counts to the higher occupancy
Coordinate fire-alarm and egress lighting for the proposed occupancy
companion_permits: Fire sprinkler permit if retrofit/relocation triggered
Fire alarm permit if devices/coverage change
citations: IEBC Chapter 10 [verify adopted edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Grease interceptor / FOG review may be required for commercial food prep
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: FOG requirements are often enforced by sewer/environmental agencies outside the normal building-permit checklist. Missing sizing or location approval can force plumbing redesign.
likely_required_actions: Size interceptor from fixture/equipment schedule
Confirm location/access with sewer or environmental authority
Show sampling port/cleanout details if required
companion_permits: FOG / industrial waste / sewer approval, if required

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seating changes require occupant-load and egress recalculation
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Adding dining, bar, queueing, or patio seats can change the required number of exits, door swing, panic hardware, travel distance, restroom counts, and fire-alarm assumptions.
likely_required_actions: Add occupant-load table by room/area
Show common path, exit access travel distance, and exit count
Coordinate exit signage/emergency lighting

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant work needs local health-department food-establishment plan review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Health-department review usually runs in parallel with the building permit and approves finish schedule, hand sinks, three-compartment sink, warewashing, refrigeration, and food-flow. Missing it can hold the certificate of occupancy even when building review is finished.
likely_required_actions: Submit food-establishment plans and equipment schedule to local health department
Show finish schedule, hand sinks, mop sink, three-compartment / warewashing, refrigeration
Coordinate health inspection before final CO
citations: FDA Food Code as adopted by local health authority [verify edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Restroom alteration can trigger ADA path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Altering a primary-function area or restroom can require upgrades to the accessible route, restrooms, drinking fountains, telephones, and parking up to the 20% disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare ADA path-of-travel cost allocation
Show accessible route to altered area
Verify restroom clearances, accessories, lavatory, mirror, and door hardware

hidden_triggers.title: Commercial alteration can require accessible-route/path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Even when the requested work is not 'an ADA project,' altered primary-function areas can require accessible route, restroom, drinking fountain, parking, and entrance upgrades within the disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare path-of-travel analysis
Budget required accessible upgrades
Document 20% disproportionality cap if used

hidden_triggers.title: Occupancy or occupant-load change can alter required restroom fixture counts
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: A retail/office/assembly change may look like tenant buildout but can require additional fixtures or accessible upgrades if occupant load or use category changes.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and plumbing-fixture-count table
Check accessible fixture requirements
Coordinate any restroom expansion with path-of-travel obligations

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant occupancy or suppression work may require fire-alarm interface review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Suppression systems, duct detectors, occupant load, and sprinkler changes can all require alarm interlocks, monitoring updates, or a separate fire-alarm submittal.
likely_required_actions: Confirm whether alarm shop drawings are deferred or separate
Show suppression/alarm interface sequence
Update monitoring account and acceptance testing
companion_permits: Fire alarm permit, if devices/interface change

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seat count can change required plumbing fixtures
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Dining, bar, patio, and assembly occupant loads can push the project over restroom fixture thresholds, affecting cost, space planning, and accessibility upgrades.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and fixture-count table
Verify male/female or all-gender fixture distribution rules
Coordinate accessible restroom clearances

_fee_floor_components.scope_note: plan review + permit + fire + utility + health
_fee_floor_components.jurisdiction_rationale: commercial plan review / fire review stack in major Texas metro [verify Dallas Development Services fees]
